content.problemStatement:
Byteland is a kingdom of islands. Alice has N maps of the different islands constituting Byteland. Map of each island consists of cities, connected by roads. Being Byteland, the road layout of each island has a binary tree structure. Each island has a capital, which is the root of the binary tree of its map. 



Alice is bored and plays the following game:

She chooses k out of these N maps and places a robot on the capital city of each map. Every robot is controlled by a common control string. If the present character of the control string is L(R), then all the robots move to the left(right) subtree on their map. All the robots follow the same control string and move on their respective maps. 



A control string is valid with respect to a given map if it corresponds to a valid path on that map. A control string is considered valid for a given set of k maps if it is valid for each of the k chosen maps. 



For example in a complete binary tree of 3 nodes, control strings "L" and "R" are valid and "LL" is an example of an invalid control string.



Alice is interested in knowing the longest control 
string which is valid for at least k maps, for each value of k. Can you find this for her?
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with an integer M, the number of maps. Then M maps description follow. The ith map description starts with an integer N, the number of cities on the island. Each of the following N-1 lines contain two space separated integers u c v (1<=u,v<=N , c=L or R) which means a road between city u and city v on the ith island and if c is L(R) then v is in the left(right) subtree of u.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output M space separated integers, where the ith integer denotes the length of the longest valid control string she can obtain assuming she can choose i maps.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a combination of tree traversal and dynamic programming. We first construct the binary tree for each map and then perform a depth-first search to find the longest valid control string for each map. We then use dynamic programming to find the longest common prefix among all possible combinations of k maps.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * M * N * 2^M) — where T is the number of test cases, M is the number of maps, and N is the number of cities on the island. The time complexity is dominated by the dynamic programming step.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(M * N) — where M is the number of maps and N is the number of cities on the island. The space complexity is dominated by the storage of the binary trees.
